title: Dynamically Corrected Bethe-Salpeter Equation Solver for Self-consistent $GW$ Reference on the Matsubara Frequency Axis
authors: Ming Wen, Gaurav Harsha, Dominika Zgid
abstract: We present a Bethe-Salpeter equation (BSE) solver based on a self-consistent $GW$ reference evaluated on the Matsubara frequency axis, referred to as BSE@sc$GW$. The self-consistent $GW$ starting point provides a robust quasiparticle description and reduces sensitivity to the initial mean-field reference compared to one-shot $GW$-based approaches. We further introduce a dynamical correction to the static Casida formulation via a plasmon-pole model. This scheme incorporates simple dynamical screening effects while retaining the efficiency of an effective eigenvalue problem. The resulting dynamically corrected BSE@sc$GW$ yields excitation energies in close agreement with high-level wavefunction-based benchmarks for both singlet and triplet excitations of small molecules. Overall, the accuracy of the dynamic BSE@sc$GW$ approach arises from the combination of a well-converged single-particle reference and the inclusion of frequency-dependent screening effects.
summary: This paper introduces a new solver for the Bethe-Salpeter Equation (BSE) that uses a self-consistent GW reference to eliminate starting-point bias. By implementing a dynamical correction via a plasmon-pole model, the authors incorporate frequency-dependent screening effects into the static Casida equation. The method is benchmarked against high-level quantum chemistry methods and shows high accuracy for singlet and triplet excitation energies in small molecules. This approach provides a robust and efficient way to study neutral excitations in molecular systems.
main_problem: The need for a computationally efficient and accurate method to calculate neutral excitation energies (optical/core excitations) while overcoming the starting-point dependence and static approximation limitations of standard GW-BSE approaches.
main_result: The development of a dynamically corrected BSE@scGW solver that achieves accuracy comparable to high-level wavefunction methods (CCSD, CC3) for small and medium-sized molecules by incorporating frequency-dependent screening.
method: A self-consistent GW (scGW) reference evaluated on the Matsubara frequency axis, combined with a dynamical correction to the static Casida formulation using a plasmon-pole model.
model_or_system: The study focuses on molecular systems, using small molecules like H2, N2, and H2O as benchmarks, within the framework of many-body perturbation theory (Hedin's equations) and the Green's function formalism.
key_observables: Singlet and triplet excitation energies, quasiparticle energies (IP, EA, fundamental gap), and excitation spectra.
important_parameters: Matsubara frequency grids (fermionic and bosonic), inverse temperature (beta), and the plasmon-pole parameters (pole location and strength).
main_assumptions: Neglect of the vertex function (Gamma approx delta), use of the quasiparticle approximation, and the adiabatic approximation (neglecting off-diagonal elements of the eigenvector matrix).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the BSE@scGW workflow; Figure 2 compares quasiparticle energy extraction methods; Figure 3 illustrates the BSE kernel Feynman diagram; Figure 4 shows H2 excitation energies vs bond length; Figure 5 and 6 demonstrate basis set convergence for water.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific problem of BSE limitations, details the BSE@scGW workflow and mathematical framework on the Matsubara axis, describes the implementation of the dynamical correction via the plasmon-pole model, presents benchmarking results against FCI and CCSD for various molecules, and discusses convergence and limitations.
why_interesting: The paper presents an advanced numerical scheme for treating frequency-dependent many-body interactions and dynamical screening, which is highly relevant to the development of accurate many-body dynamics and spectroscopic simulations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. In counterclockwise order, the workflow of BSE@scGW proceeds as follows: (a) It begins with a mean-field calculation (HF in this study). (b) The density-fitted integrals and resulting matrices are then passed into the scGW cycle and iterated until self-consistency is reached. (c) The scGW quasiparticle energy levels are extracted using the QP approximation. (d) These QP energies, together with the corresponding output matrices, are subsequently used in the static BSE Casida equation. (e) The resulting static BSE solution is then dynamically corrected through plasmon-pole fitting. The shorthand “a.u.” stands for atomic unit. (f) In the final step, the dynamically corrected...

Figure 2. Charged excitations of N2 calculated at the scGW/aug-cc-pVTZ level of theory. The negative and posi- tive half-axes contain the ionization potential (IP) peaks and electronic affinity (EA) peaks respectively. The QP energy levels serve as the initial inputs for the subsequent BSE cal- culation shown in FIG. 1 (e) and (f).

Figure 3. Feynman diagram of the particle-hole Bethe– Salpeter equation (see Eqn. (16)). Subscripts signify valence and conduction orbitals. BSE kernel Ξ is approximated as the sum of the electron-hole exchange term U and the electron- hole attraction term −W in Eqn. (27).

Figure 4. (a) The first four singlet excitations and (b) the first four triplet excitations of H2 molecule calculated with dynamically corrected BSE@scGW. (c) The ground states calculated with scGW. Black curves are reference FCI results. Both BSE@scGW and FCI calculations employ cc-pVTZ basis set. The equilibrium bond length (0.74 ˚A) is indicated by vertical dashed lines.
Figure 5. Convergent behavior of water QP energy levels (HOMO−1, HOMO, LUMO, LUMO+1). Calculated with scGW using cc-pVXZ (X = D, T, Q, 5, 6) and aug-cc-pVXZ (X = D, T, Q, 5) basis sets.

Figure 6. Convergent behavior of the first three water singlet excitations (1B1, 1A2, and 1A1). Calculated with dynamically corrected BSE@scGW, using cc-pVXZ (X = D, T, Q, 5, 6) and aug-cc-pVXZ (X = D, T, Q, 5) basis sets.

Fig. 6 shows how three singlet excitation energies ob- tained with BSE@scGW converge for the two different basis set families. The corresponding numerical values are provided in Table S1 of the Supplementary Material. For the cc-pVXZ series, since the electron-hole energy differences enter as inputs in the BSE Hamiltonian, this instability in QP energy levels propagates directly into the excitation energies, leading to the poor convergence observed in Fig. 6. By contrast, aug-cc-pVXZ basis sets better represent both the spatially extended virtual or- bitals and the associated screening effects in W.
